model_input:
Question: A 12-year-old boy with no relevant medical history presented with a left maxillary gingival swelling of 4 years  duration associated with palatal tipping of the left maxillary central incisor. The lesion reportedly grew over a 2-year period but remained unchanged over the past 2 years. The patient denied pain, bleeding, or neurosensory disturbances associated with the lesion or dentition. Intraoral examination was notable for a firm, 1 1-cm, pink, dome-shaped gingival mass extending onto the facial surface of tooth number 9, coalescing with a broader, pale-appearing, soft to firm gingival enlargement apical to the tooth (Figure, A). No edema, erythema, or signs of infection were appreciated, and the adjacent teeth demonstrated no mobility with normal periodontal probing depths and vitality. Extraoral examination was unremarkable. Radiographic examination showed no endodontic involvement or intralesional calcifications; however, cone-beam computed tomography imaging revealed saucerization of the facial bone near tooth number 9.A, Large, rounded submucosal lesion of the anterior maxilla facial and apical to tooth number 9. B, Low-power histomicrograph shows a nodule of fibrous connective tissue surfaced by keratinized stratified squamous epithelium (original magnification  5). The bulk of the specimen consists of densely to loosely arranged collagen bundles with scattered fibroblasts. Centrally, there is a focal proliferation of odontogenic epithelium (pink arrowhead). The inset shows a higher-power image with a proliferation of odontogenic epithelium composed of hyperchromatic ovoid cells that are arranged into pseudoductal structures (blue arrowheads; original magnification  20). Small basophilic calcifications also are identified within the epithelial proliferation (black arrowhead).Excisional biopsy of the lesion was performed, and microscopic examination of multiple sections revealed a nodule of keratinized superficial stratified squamous epithelium overlying fibrous connective tissue (Figure, B). The surface epithelium demonstrated a normal maturational pattern. The underlying fibrous connective tissue was composed of densely to loosely arranged collagen bundles interspersed with fibroblasts, blood vessels, and scattered chronic inflammatory cells. Notably, within the central portions of the fibrous nodule on deeper levels, there was a proliferation of odontogenic epithelium composed of mildly hyperchromatic ovoid cells arranged into pseudoducts (Figure, B). Small basophilic spherical calcifications were intermixed with the odontogenic epithelium. What Is Your Diagnosis?
Options: A:Peripheral ossifying fibroma B:Peripheral ameloblastoma C:Peripheral adenomatoid odontogenic tumor D:Lymphoma
 Which option is the correct answer to the question? ALWAYS provide the option index of the final answer AT THE END with TRIPLE BACKTICKS among A, B, C, D, EXACTLY like this: The answer is ```X```. DO NOT add any other text or variations.
explanation: Adenomatoid odontogenic tumor (AOT) is a noninvasive odontogenic tumor with approximately two-thirds associated with impacted canine teeth in the anterior maxilla and a female:male predilection of 1.9:1; as such, it is often referred to as the two-thirds tumor. Typically occurring in the second decade of life, an AOT originates from remnants of the dental lamina or enamel organ and is associated with mutations in the KRAS gene (mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway; p.G12V and p.G12R loci) in 70% of AOTs according to recent genetic data published in the fifth edition of the World Health Organization s classification of odontogenic and maxillofacial bone tumors.1,2 While 97.7% of all AOTs fall into either the follicular (71%) or extrafollicular (29%) subtypes of the central variant, a peripheral AOT (PAOT) is rare but possible, with 2.3% of all AOTs solely involving the gingival tissue overlying the alveolar ridge.3 The PAOT also demonstrates a much higher female predilection, with a reported female:male ratio of 6.3:1.4 The pathogenesis of a PAOT involves proliferation of epithelial remnants within the gingival tissue following tooth eruption in response to an unknown stimulus.Radiographically, follicular and extrafollicular AOTs appear as well-defined, circumscribed, and unilocular radiolucencies associated with unerupted teeth with varying degrees of calcification. As a PAOT occurs in soft tissue, there are no pathognomonic radiographic findings; however, erosion of the labial plate and displacement of teeth, as seen in this case, as well as deep periodontal pockets are not uncommon.5 Histologically, both central AOTs and PAOTs demonstrate ductlike structures arising from the cuboidal epithelial cell component of the lesion, with spindle-shaped epithelial cells interspersed with cementumlike calcifications or amyloidlike homogeneous eosinophilic material. Typically, AOTs are well encapsulated with connective tissue and, as such, are easily separated from adjacent anatomic structures; however, this encapsulation is usually not present in PAOTs.6-8 Due to their similar pathogenesis and cellular origin, many reactive proliferation lesions, including peripheral ossifying fibroma, peripheral giant cell granuloma, and pyogenic granuloma, as well as many peripheral odontogenic cysts or tumors, including peripheral ameloblastoma, odontogenic fibroma, gingival cyst, peripheral ameloblastic fibroma, peripheral odontoma, peripheral odontogenic keratocyst, and peripheral calcifying odontogenic cyst, can demonstrate similar gingival enlargement as seen with PAOTs.6-8 As a result of multiple lesions appearing similar clinically, definitive diagnosis is made only after histopathological examination.Treatment for a PAOT typically involves conservative surgical removal via enucleation and curettage of the lesion down to bone, resulting in very rare instances of recurrence of the tumor, with excellent prognosis.6,9 The present patient was followed up at 1 month at the Department of Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery at the University of Illinois Chicago College of Dentistry, and subsequently at 6 months at the patient s initial referring dentist, with no evidence of lesion recurrence. While no appreciable spontaneous buccal movement of the malpositioned left maxillary lateral incisor was observed at 1-month follow-up, the patient was cleared to initiate orthodontic intervention.This case highlights the unique nature of odontogenic lesions to occur throughout the oral cavity and consideration of a broad differential diagnosis, because even benign-appearing lesions could potentially represent devastating diseases. Additionally, early diagnosis and intervention helped in limiting underlying bone loss and further displacement of tooth number 9, demonstrating that early detection and biopsy play important roles in therapeutic success.

raw_response:
Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this 12-year-old boy with a left maxillary gingival swelling. Let me go through the information step by step.

First, the patient has a 4-year history of a left maxillary gingival swelling. It grew over 2 years but hasn't changed in the last 2 years. So, it's been stable for a while. He doesn't have any pain, bleeding, or neurosensory issues, which suggests it's not something aggressive like a cancer or infection.

Looking at the intraoral exam, there's a firm, 1 cm pink dome-shaped mass on the facial surface of tooth 9. It's coalescing with a broader, pale soft to firm enlargement apical to the tooth. No edema, erythema, or infection signs. The adjacent teeth are fine, no mobility, normal probing depths, and they're vital. So, it's not affecting the teeth much.

The radiographic exam shows no endodontic issues or calcifications, but the cone-beam CT shows saucerization of the facial bone near tooth 9. That makes me think there's some bone involvement, maybe a lesion eroding into the bone.

The biopsy results are key here. The lesion is a nodule of keratinized stratified squamous epithelium over fibrous connective tissue. The surface epithelium is normal in maturation. Underneath, there's fibrous tissue with some inflammation. But the deeper part has a proliferation of odontogenic epithelium, which is arranged into pseudoducts. There are also small basophilic calcifications mixed in.

So, what's this? Let me think about the options:

A: Peripheral ossifying fibroma. That's a benign tumor, usually in the jaw, made of fibrous tissue with calcifications. It's often on the outer side of the tooth, but the description here includes odontogenic epithelium, which I don't think is part of ossifying fibroma.

B: Peripheral ameloblastoma. This is a benign tumor arising from the ameloblasts, the cells that form the dentin. It's usually under the gingiva, on the outer aspect of the tooth. It can have a pseudoductal structure, which matches the histology here with the odontogenic epithelium in pseudoducts and calcifications. That fits.

C: Peripheral adenomatoid odontogenic tumor. This is another benign tumor, but it's more lobular and less likely to have the pseudoductal pattern. It's usually associated with more adenomatoid features, which I don't see here.

D: Lymphoma. That's a possibility, but the patient's history is stable for 2 years, and the biopsy doesn't show lymphoid cells. Plus, the presence of odontogenic epithelium and calcifications makes it more likely a tumor related to tooth development.

So, putting it all together, the histology points towards a peripheral ameloblastoma because of the pseudoductal structures and odontogenic origin. The saucerization on CT also supports a benign, slow-growing lesion.
</think>

The patient's lesion is a stable, firm, pink mass with histological features of keratinized stratified squamous epithelium over fibrous tissue and a proliferation of odontogenic epithelium forming pseudoducts. This aligns with the characteristics of a peripheral ameloblastoma, a benign tumor arising from ameloblasts.

The answer is ```B```
